text: Good morning, my name is Malcolm West. I'm an NIHR clinical academic working in the University of Southampton and I'm also a general surgical colorectal trainee in Wessex in the UK.
Within the next half hour I'll be talking to you about cardiopulmonary exercise testing or CPET and prehabilitation in the perioperative setting.

So there are about four million surgical procedures performed annually in the United Kingdom with a various morbidity and mortality ranging up to twenty-five percent in morbidity. Twelve percent of these patients are already labelled as high-risk individuals and eighty-three percent of those are responsible for total mortality and length of hospital stay.

When considering rectal cancer resections, published morbidity can be up to forty percent. When looking at epidemiological literature, there is a very strong link between surgical morbidity and reduced survival. In the surgical literature, however, there is poor reporting for this morbidity. So for us surgeons and perioperative physicians, the major challenge is to identify these high-risk individuals and optimise their post-operative course.

So when looking at a cardiopulmonary exercise test, this is a figure taken from Principles and Practices of Exercise Physiology edited by Carl Wasserman from UCLA and essentially it shows that we measure oxygen uptake and oxygen and carbon dioxide output at the mouth using a cardiopulmonary exercise test. And this is tracked all the way down the oxygen cascade into the mitochondria, which are the site of interest.

And this test has been popularised in the nineteen nineties by Professor Paul Older in Australia. And it is now a gold standard to link in physical fitness and stratify risk for perioperative outcome.

In his seminal paper, Paul Older in Cheston of nineteen ninety three, he said that low physical fitness determined by CPET is linked to poor patient outcome. As you can see from the picture, this is an elderly gentleman with advanced rectal cancer waiting for his operation.

He is undergoing a CPET test and he is currently pedaling on an exercise bike and is attached to non-invasive blood pressure monitoring, arterial saturations, a full-twell VDCG and a tight-fitting mask. to measure his oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide output.

So CPET is defined as a non-invasive graded exercise challenge which is performed on a cycle ergometer. It can also be performed on an arm crank or a treadmill. However, in patients who are waiting for an operation, I think cycle ergometry is much better as it's weight supported.

This allows the objective assessment of physical fitness and at rest and under stress, mimicking the stress of major surgery. And in the literature in the last ten years there have been a flurry of papers looking at the optimal predictors between different surgical types.

We've looked at the oxygen uptake at lactic threshold or the anaerobic threshold with the magic number of eleven milligrams per kilo per minute. in major intra-abdominal surgery. We've also looked at the oxygen uptake at peak and the ventilator equivalents for carbon dioxide, which are indicative of a peripartive risk and can stratify according to outcome.

So what is prehabilitation? Prehabilitation has been popularised by the Montreal group, the Carly group in Canada, and this is a form of exercise training that is aimed to prevent injuries before they actually happen.

We prehabilitate from before major surgery, before thoracic, abdominal or orthopaedic nowadays and after neoadjuvant cancer therapies. And why prehabilitate? I mean we traditionally as surgeons we have focused on the post-operative recovery process and intervening after and around the major surgical event using the enhanced recovery pathways and this is one of the paper one of the papers from the Monson Group in Hull, looking at a systematic review and meta-analysis of this.

However, the pre-operative period may be a more emotionally salient time to intervene as patients can be actively engaged in their operative recovery and in their operation. Rehabilitation has been done for decades in heart failure patients, in post-MI patients, and also in respiratory patients, post-COPD patients. However, the evidence is quite scanty.

We have published a review in Best Practice in Research Clinical Anesthesiology in two thousand and eleven and we have published a systematic review in the BJA of two thousand and thirteen looking at this evidence. These are two other papers by Valknit in two thousand and ten and by Singh in two thousand and fourteen in Western Australia looking at the presurgical exercise interventions in a cancer population.

This is one of our tables in our recent BJA paper that shows there are very small number of patients in about ten randomised control or observation trials with a number of about five hundred and twenty patients. These studies we thought were badly designed, lacked power, however their intervention improved fitness.

And why have we been doing this in the UK? This is my experience that we've been doing prehabilitations since the year ten and this is the experience of the Fitness for Surgery Consortium led by Professor Mike Grocott in Southampton.

Our aim was initially to look at CPET variables and predict the in-hospital morbidity in patients undergoing major colonic and rectal surgeries. We have different papers which I'll show you looking at colonic surgery both benign and malignant, rectal cancer surgery and colorectal and upper GI cancer patients after their chemotherapy interventions.

This is one of our first papers, the colonic surgery paper. These are a hundred and thirty-three patients, both benign and malignant group. And as you can see from the receiver operator curves, the cuts, the cutoff for oxygen uptake at lactic threshold and at peak are similar to the published literature, around about ten or eleven. for the anaerobic threshold and sixteen for peak which have a good sensitivity and specificity and relate to in-hospital short-term outcome.

We then looked at a risk prediction model for these colorectal patients and we can see that if you're a male you have a four and a half percent chance of morbidity post-surgery If you have an oxygen uptake of around eleven and you are able to increase that by one point, you can reduce your odds ratio by twenty-five percent, so your risk declines by twenty-five percent. If you are able to improve that by two milligrams per kilo per minute, your risk of inhospital morbidity falls by about forty percent. The operation type is also important. And with patients having an open resection, this goes up by twofold. We can look at a multivariable model, and the risk prediction model is currently now used in Aintree Hospital in Liverpool, based on gender, anaerobic threshold, and operation type. And this gives a better sensitivity and specificity, and a better prediction when looking at in-hospital morbidity.

This is the paper from the BJA in which I encourage you to read. When looking at rectal cancer surgery, these are rectal cancer patients only with having a major surgical operation. And as you can see, the sensitivity and specificity around the measurements is much increased, so the CPET test is more predictive in rectal cancer surgery, we think.

This is another risk prediction model which we use and based around the anaerobic threshold and the oxygen uptake at peak and these are quite staggering numbers so we're saying here that if you are above the median of eleven mils per kilo per minute in your anaerobic threshold your odds ratio or your risk declines by about ninety five percent whilst if you can improve your VO two peak by one point your risk reduction is twenty-five percent.

And this is reflected by the Kaplan-Meier curve for length of hospital stay, in that if you have a complication, your length of stay is much longer, almost double, as opposed to people having no complications. And this is the paper, the citation from the BJS in two thousand and fourteen.

What about patients undergoing neoadjuvant cancer treatment? So we've looked at two groups. This is an upper GI neoadjuvant chemotherapy group. These are thirty-eight patients tested both pre chemotherapy and post chemotherapy. and having a six week course of platinum based chemotherapy compounds. And as you can see, there is a clinically and statistically significant reduction in their physical fitness by about two milligrams per kilo per minute, which is quite acute in a six week period.

If you can look at the Kaplan-Meier plots, if you are a fit group above the magic number of fourteen in this case, you will live for longer as opposed to being below the cut-off point at one year. This is the paper reflecting all the upper GI data published in the European Journal of Surgical Oncology in

We've done exactly the same with neoadjuvant chemotherapy in locally advanced rectal cancer patients and we've tested them pre and post chemotherapy within a six week interval. These patients received a standardised course of chemotherapy And all these patients were T-tree and plus circumferential resection margin-threatened cancer patients. So quite advanced disease, however operable. And we've seen exactly the same thing that the lactate threshold or the anaerobic threshold has fallen by one point five mils, which is both clinically and statistically significant. And we've also published this paper in the European Journal of Surgical Oncology in two thousand and fourteen for your reference.

The take-home messages from our Fitness for Surgery consortium are that oxygen uptake at lactic threshold or the anaerobic threshold and the oxygen uptake at peak exercise are independently related to five-day post-operative morbidity, so in-hospital short-term outcome. fitness declines with neoadjuvant cancer treatments, and these data are taken to be unique, strongly clinically meaningful and attainable when considering the use of a prehabilitation program in major cancer surgery. So how have we been doing this?

This is our prehabilitation experience from the Fitness for Surgery Collaborative. This was well taken up by the BBC and all these citations are freely available on the web. These are very good articles published by the BBC World Service and I urge you to read them. They have quotes from a couple of our patients which were quite emotionally touching. As you can see also the Royal College of Surgeons both in Edinburgh and in England have launched their exercise campaign to reduce mortality by fivefold, which I think is very salient in this climate.

This is a recent press release from the University of Southampton looking at our prehabilitation programme. So when considering the rectal cancer rehabilitation programme, so my work in Liverpool and in Southampton, our aim was to evaluate the effect of neoadjuvant chemotherapy and a six-week structured responsive exercise training programme on CPET outcomes, namely VO-to-atlantic threshold, in these locally advanced rectal cancer patients.

These are the methods, as you can see. These are thirty-nine patients which were all They all underwent a CPET test at baseline. Within a week, they underwent standardised long-course chemotherapy therapy for six weeks. After that, they immediately undertook a CPET test again, which was considered week zero. They were then split into an exercise and a control group in a non-randomised control way. The exercise group included twenty-two patients, the control group included thirteen, and they had exercise versus control for a six-week group, for a six-week period, and were followed up until their surgery at week fifteen. All these patients had standardised chemotherapy and had a standardised T-n plus circumferential resection margin threatened cancer. This is the training program.

These patients underwent eighteen sessions with three CPET tests embedded within the training program. The training program lasted for about forty minutes, three days a week for six weeks. And the adherence of this group was phenomenal, which was ninety-four percent for all training sessions and CPET sessions with zero adverse events. This is a more in-depth look at the exercise training program. This was an interval training program with moderate and severe intensities based on the cardiopulmonary exercise test at week zero. So this was all tailored medicine. And the patients went exercising in the moderate and severe strength intensity based around the lactate threshold. These are the visual outputs from the exercise bike. This is an Ergoline Optibike.

It's essentially a bike that you can find in a gym. However, you can preload an exercise training programme on a chip and pin card. This is the visual output that the patient looks at on completing their exercise programme with heart rate as an undulating baseline and the blocks of workload in the background. These are the patient characteristics for the rectal cancer patients. As you can see the control group was older, were considered to be sicker, and had a WHO performance status which was worse than the exercise group.

This is a busy slide. We're looking at all the outputs from the cardiopulmonary exercise test. And if you can look at the column on the right, which shows the level of significance, this is a compound symmetry covariance analysis, which looks at the individual time points and compares pre-chemotherapy versus week zero versus week six with one p-value.

And as you can see, we had statistically significant changes in anaerobic threshold, oxygen uptake at peak, O-pulse at lactic threshold, O-pulse at peak, workload both at lactic threshold and at peak, and we have also found a significant reduction in heart rate both at rest and at peak exercise. This is a graphic interpretation of that data looking at oxygen uptake at lactic threshold between pre-chemotherapy, post-chemotherapy, and week six. And as you can see, patients declined. in their fitness losing about one point nine mils per kilo per minute. However, within six weeks that was regained and the exercise group, which is the dashed line, went back to the original baseline. However, the control group kept losing fitness.

And exactly the same thing happened when considering oxygen uptake at peak. This is the change in fitness over the whole exercise program between week zero and week fourteen. You can see that patients improved as early as week three in the exercise group. And there was already a positive inflection in the exercise group as compared to the control group. The exercise group undulated and went back to baseline at week fourteen when we operated on these patients. However, the control group unfortunately kept on losing their fitness and had a fitness which was predicted to be predictive of complications. These are individual patient data.

On the left you can see the oxygen uptake atlactic threshold between post chemotherapy and week six in the control group. And on the right you can see the exact same for the exercise group. As you can see, you can split these patients into responders and non-responders. There are either patients who have a good response to exercise or patients who don't have a good response to exercise. And maybe that's linked to the exercise challenge that we've provided them with.

This is a physical activity graph. We've given these patients a triaxial accelerometer, activity monitor, measuring all sorts of variables. However, the best variable to look at is the mean number of steps, which has declined between pre and post chemotherapy. As you can see, these patients are baseline unfit with about five thousand steps per day. as opposed to healthy human beings which should have a step count of about ten thousand steps per day. This has dropped between pre and post chemotherapy and as you can see both control group and the exercise group have regained their original baseline activity. However, this is interesting when compared to the oxygen uptake CPET variables in that the exercise stimulus provided to the exercise group is the only accountable force that drives their improvement in physical fitness and not their physical activity.

You can read all this in the British Journal of Anesthesia in the February edition published in So what is the clinical relevance of all this? I think these patients get a change in behaviour towards exercise, which is well documented in both of our papers when looking at their quality of life, both objectively and subjectively. We have also looked at the effects of neoadjuvant chemotherapy in the same cohort on mitochondrial function. This is a novel way of looking at in vivo mitochondrial function, using something called a thirty one phosphorus magnetic resonance spectroscopy or thirty one PMRS for short. and whole body CPET measures.

We are the first group that has ever done this in a cancer population. As you can see from the pictures on the right, this patient has rectal cancer and is exercising within the bore of a three Tesla MR scanner with a phosphorous coil strapped to his right leg. And the phosphorous coil gives us something called KPCR, or phosphocreatinine recovery time constant, which is an in vivo measure of mitochondrial function. This can be easily related to oxygen uptake, both at peak and at anaerobic threshold. And as you can see, this is a randomized controlled trial. of N equals twelve and we've split these patients into the exercise group and the control group with exactly the same exercise stimulus as previously. And you can see that the KPCR has declined between pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy. which correlates very nicely to their VO-II at peak and their lactate threshold. When these patients were split into the exercise group and the control group, you can see that on the left in the exercise group, these patients have restored both their whole body oxygen uptake levels, and this has mirrored quite nicely with their KPCR function. So their mitochondrial function, within a very short period of time, has declined and then overshot the baseline. as opposed to the control group who haven't had any training and their KPCR is almost exactly the same, which mirrors quite nicely the oxygen uptake variables.

And you can read this in a recently published paper in PLOS ONE in So in conclusion, preoperative exercise training is a valuable intervention to improve physical fitness post chemotherapy prior to major elective cancer surgery. Preoperative exercise training with different intensities and durations needs to be explored further. added benefits and clinical relevance is the change of behaviour that these patients have towards exercise in the long term, their improvement in quality of life and their improvement in activity levels which we have measured. This may have important implications in improving surgical outcome And we're looking at this using an upper GI cancer cohort and a lower GI cancer cohort, both observational and randomised controlled. This is NIHR funded and hopefully the results will be out in as the trials will end this year.

And there is a need to explore potential mechanisms for this decline in physical fitness with neoadjuvant cancer treatments and the improvement with prehabilitation also needs to be explored as we have no idea what the mechanistic driving force is behind that. These are my acknowledgements. I would like to thank the Fitness for Surgery Consortium, the University of Southampton, our funding partners which are the NIA and the National Institute for Health Research. Thank you very much for listening.
